# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:** AI chatbots do not have the same level of insight or familiarity with CSD3/Dawn as the human support team who maintain these systems.
* **Verify before you act:** Do not copy and run commands or instructions provided by AI models without review. Be especially cautious with:
  + Suggestions to use `sudo` commands or any other privilege escalation routes
  + Software installation steps that may be inappropriate or incompatible with our hardware/software stack(s)
  + Requests for login or sensitive credentials
* **Be security conscious:**
  + **Be aware of data retention:** Most AI assistants/websites retain or process data externally. Anything you share could be stored or used to improve the model; treat prompts as public information.
  + **Avoid sharing system-specific details:** Do not include configuration files, system paths, network layouts, or architecture diagrams that could expose internal infrastructure details.
  + **Do not upload logs or files directly:** These may contain sensitive metadata, user data, or internal identifiers that should remain confidential.

Tip

If you need to illustrate a problem, replace identifying information (e.g., usernames, project codes, or directory paths) with placeholders such as `<user>`, `<project>`, or `<path>`.

* **Protect your information:** Never include sensitive or identifying data in your prompts. This includes:
  + Account names
  + Passwords
  + SSH/API keys or other sensitive passcodes
  + IP addresses or hostnames
  + Any data protected under institutional, legal or intellectual property confidentiality policies

Attention

**If you think you have shared sensitive information:** make sure to reset the exposed credentials and let us know by contacting support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. In your e-mail, please provide a description of what happened, including as much relevant information as possible.

### What Can I Do?[¶](#what-can-i-do "Permalink to this headline")

The following items should help offer some clarity on how to engage with the CSD3/Dawn support team. Please consult and follow this advice.

1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.
2. **Be explicit:** use a descriptive subject line. In the body of your email, provide as much relevant information as possible and avoid pasting errors or log files without context. Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.
4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical issues.
5. **Contact us

# Research Computing Services HPC Documentation[¶](#research-computing-services-hpc-documentation "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* Please read [this page](/hpc/data_centre_upgrade.html) for important information about the data centre upgrade work and expected disruptions from June 2025 until December 2026.

The [Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)](https://www.csd3.cam.ac.uk) is one of the [EPSRC](http://www.epsrc.ac.uk)
Tier2 National HPC Services hosted by [Research Computing Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk) at the [University of Cambridge](https://www.cam.ac.uk).

The facility became live in November 2017 and began the next chapter in HPC for the University of Cambridge. It was upgraded
in 2020, and again in 2021 and 2023.

CSD3 is currently a combination of two platform types:

* Intel Xeon Scalable Processors (Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids CPUs)
* NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

All CSD3 nodes are currently interconnected by Mellanox HDR
InfiniBand. The GPU sub-cluster is also known as Wilkes3-GPU in its
own right. The name “Peta4” was associated historically with the
original CPU elements of CSD3.

CSD3 is available to academic researchers both internal and external to Cambridge, and to industry. For
more information please see the [website](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/high-performance-computing).

The version that it loads is 4.3.1, whereas I would like 4.5.1. Is it possible to get the interactive Rstudio to load this R version?
Thank you!
Best,
Heather
–
Heather Machado
Group Leader
CRUK Career Development Fellow
Department of Pathology
Department of Genetics
University of Cambridge
Cambridge, UK
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Login-Web / RStudio Service Configuration
Technical Action Required: No
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user wants Login-Web RStudio to use R 4.5.1 instead of the currently configured 4.3.1. The safest answer is to say that this is not currently possible with the Login-Web RStudio service as configured and to recommend alternative workflows where appropriate.

ACTION
1) Confirm that Login-Web RStudio cannot presently be switched to R 4.5.1.
2) Do not promise a timeline for service reconfiguration.
3) Offer an alternative such as Jupyter or a non-Login-Web workflow if the user needs a newer R version.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Dear Heather,

It is not currently possible to make the Login-Web RStudio service use R 4.5.1. The service is constrained by the way it is configured.

If you need that newer R version immediately, please use an alternative workflow such as a different interactive environment rather than Login-Web RStudio.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user requires a newer version of R (4.5.1) for RStudio interactive sessions. The current version available is 4.3.1. There is no direct evidence of version 4.5.1 being available or the process to request it.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the availability of R version 4.5.1 on the system.
2. If available, provide instructions to load the specific version.
3. If not available, escalate to the appropriate team to check the feasibility of installing the requested version.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Is there a specific reason or feature in R 4.5.1 that is required for your work?
- Are there any alternative versions of R that could meet your needs?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Heather,

Thank you for your inquiry. We will need to verify the availability of R version 4.5.1 on our system. If it is available, we will provide you with the necessary instructions to load it. If not, we will escalate your request to the appropriate team to check the feasibility of installing the requested version.

Please let us know if there is a specific reason or feature in R 4.5.1 that is required for your work, or if there are any alternative versions of R that could meet your needs.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any new software versions requested comply with the system's compatibility and security policies.
- Document any changes or updates made to the system for future reference.

REFERENCE KEY

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

to `~/.R/Makevars`.

Login-Web](loginweb-apps-jupyter.html#jupyter-access-loginweb)
8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

#### Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the jupyter notebook app. Select the kernel to be deleted, then type:

   ```
   jupyter kernelspec uninstall <myenv>
   ```

### RStudio server: Csd3[¶](#rstudio-server-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is an open-source, integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It includes a console, syntax-highlighting editor that supports direct code execution, and tools for plotting, history, debugging, and workspace management.

#### Starting RStudio on CSD3[¶](#starting-rstudio-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

From the `Interactive Apps` dropdown, select `RStudio server: Csd3`. This should open a job form as shown in the following image:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-start.png)

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-newproject.png)

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-session.png)

#### Further Reading:[¶](#further-reading "Permalink to this headline")

## Reference[¶](#reference "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web is based on [Open OnDemand](https://openondemand.org/)

This documentation is based on Ohio Supercomputer Center user documentation for their Open OnDemand deployment (OSC OnDemand).

More detail can be found at:

### App Developers[¶](#app-developers "Permalink to this headline")

If you’d like to develop your own app through OnDemand, see the link below for detailed instructions.

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

to `~/.R/Makevars`.

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Rstudio interactive session issue

[CONTEXT]
Many thanks, another question if I may: is there any way to use a specific version of R (e.g. something newer than 4.4.0) in the interactive rstudio sessions?
J

Login-Web](loginweb-apps-jupyter.html#jupyter-access-loginweb)
8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

#### Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the jupyter notebook app. Select the kernel to be deleted, then type:

   ```
   jupyter kernelspec uninstall <myenv>
   ```

### RStudio server: Csd3[¶](#rstudio-server-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is an open-source, integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It includes a console, syntax-highlighting editor that supports direct code execution, and tools for plotting, history, debugging, and workspace management.

#### Starting RStudio on CSD3[¶](#starting-rstudio-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

From the `Interactive Apps` dropdown, select `RStudio server: Csd3`. This should open a job form as shown in the following image:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-start.png)

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-newproject.png)

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-session.png)

#### Further Reading:[¶](#further-reading "Permalink to this headline")

## Reference[¶](#reference "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web is based on [Open OnDemand](https://openondemand.org/)

This documentation is based on Ohio Supercomputer Center user documentation for their Open OnDemand deployment (OSC OnDemand).

More detail can be found at:

### App Developers[¶](#app-developers "Permalink to this headline")

If you’d like to develop your own app through OnDemand, see the link below for detailed instructions.

# Dawn - Machine Learning & Data Science frameworks[¶](#dawn-machine-learning-data-science-frameworks "Permalink to this headline")

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## TensorFlow[¶](#tensorflow "Permalink to this headline")

Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate tensorflow-gpu
```

To run on the PVCs, there should be no need to modify your code - the Intel
optimised implementation will run automatically on the GPU, assuming it has been
installed as intel-extension-for-tensorflow[xpu].

## Jax/OpenXLA[¶](#jax-openxla "Permalink to this headline")

Documentation can be found on GitHub: [Intel OpenXLA](https://github.com/intel/intel-extension-for-openxla)

## Julia[¶](#julia "Permalink to this headline")

This is currently known not to work correctly on PVC GPUs. (Mar 2024)

# Running jobs on Dawn[¶](#running-jobs-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. Additionally Dawn project names follow the pattern NAME-DAWN-GPU.

> The pvc-s nodes have **96 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and 1024 GiB of RAM. This
> means that Slurm will allocate **24 cpus per GPU**.

## Recommendations for running on Dawn[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

The resource limits are currently set to a maximum of 64 GPUs per user with a
maximum wallclock time of 36 hours per job.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Dawn[¶](#default-submission-script-for-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

A template submission script will be provided soon.

To submit a job to the Dawn PVC partition, your batch script should look similar
to the following example:

```
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --job-name=my-batch-job
#SBATCH --account=<your Dawn SLURM project>
#SBATCH --partition=pvc9 # Dawn PVC partition
#SBATCH -n 4   # Number of tasks (usually number of MPI ranks)
#SBATCH -c 24  # Number of cores per task
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 # Number of requested GPUs per node

module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
# Set up environment below for example by loading more modules

srun <your_application>
```

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p pvc9
```

### Jobs requiring multiple nodes[¶](#jobs-requiring-multiple-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

Multi-node jobs need to request 4 GPUs per node, i.e.:

```
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using the [Intel MPI Library](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/mpi-library.html) provided by the oneAPI toolkit:

```
module av intel-oneapi-mpi
```

To use GPU Aware MPI and allow passing device buffers to MPI calls, set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD`
environment variable to `1` in your submission script:

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD=1
```

If you are sure that your code only involves buffers of the same type (e.g.
only GPU buffers or only host buffers) in a single
MPI operation you can further optimize MPI communication between GPUs by setting

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD_SYMMETRIC=1
```

This will disable handling of MPI communication between GPU buffers and host buffers.

### Multithreading jobs[¶](#multithreading-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

If your code uses multithreading (e.g. host-based OpenMP), you will need to
specify the number of threads per process in your Slurm batch script using the
cores-per-task parameter. For example, to run a hybrid MPI-OpenMP application
using 24 processes and 4 threads per task:

```
#SBATCH -n 24 # or --ntasks
#SBATCH -c 4 # or --cores-per-task
```

If you do *not* specify the cores-per-task parameter Slurm will pin the
threads to the same core, reducing performance.

### Recommended Compilers[¶](#recommended-compilers "Permalink to this headline")

We recommend using the [Intel oneAPI](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/overview.html) compilers

[https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/articles/technical/accelerate-stable-diffusion-on-intel-gpus-openxla.html)
It would be great if I could have some support in doing this, as I think our group and other potential JAX/DAWN users will benefit greatly from this.
Many thanks,
Lewis

# Dawn - Machine Learning & Data Science frameworks[¶](#dawn-machine-learning-data-science-frameworks "Permalink to this headline")

We provide a set of pre-populated Conda environments based on the Intel
Distribution for Python:

```
module av intelpython-conda
conda info -e
```

This module provides environments for PyTorch and Tensorflow.

Please note that Intel code and documentation sometimes refers to ‘XPUs’, a more
generic term for accelerators, GPU or otherwise. For Dawn, ‘XPU’ and ‘GPU’ can
usually be considered interchangeable.

## PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch on Intel GPUs is supported by the [Intel Extension for PyTorch](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-pytorch/xpu/latest/).
On Dawn this version of PyTorch is accessible as a conda environment named pytorch-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate pytorch-gpu
```

Adapting your code to run on the PVCs is straightforward and only takes a few
lines of code. For details, see the official documentation - but as a quick
example:

```
import torch
import intel_extension_for_pytorch as ipex

...

# Enable GPU
model = model.to('xpu')
data = data.to('xpu')

model = ipex.optimize(model, dtype=torch.float32)
```

## TensorFlow[¶](#tensorflow "Permalink to this headline")

Intel supports optimised TensorFlow on both CPU and GPU, using the [Intel
Extension for TensorFlow](https://intel.github.io/intel-extension-for-tensorflow/latest/get_started.html).
On Dawn this version on TensorFlow is accessible as a conda environment named tensorflow-gpu:

```
module load intelpython-conda
conda activate tensorflow-gpu
```

To run on the PVCs, there should be no need to modify your code - the Intel
optimised implementation will run automatically on the GPU, assuming it has been
installed as intel-extension-for-tensorflow[xpu].

## Jax/OpenXLA[¶](#jax-openxla "Permalink to this headline")

Documentation can be found on GitHub: [Intel OpenXLA](https://github.com/intel/intel-extension-for-openxla)

## Julia[¶](#julia "Permalink to this headline")

This is currently known not to work correctly on PVC GPUs. (Mar 2024)

# Running jobs on Dawn[¶](#running-jobs-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

## Software[¶](#software "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC nodes run [Rocky Linux 9](https://rockylinux.org/), which is a rebuild of Red Hat
Enterprise Linux 9 (RHEL9). The Sapphire Rapids CPUs on these
nodes are also more modern and support newer instructions than most other CSD3
partitions.

To ensure your environment is clean and set up correctly for Dawn, please purge your modules and load the
base Dawn environment:

```
module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
```

As we provide a separate set of modules specifically for dawn nodes, in
general, we don’t support running software built for other CSD3 partitions on
Dawn nodes. Therefore you are strongly recommended to rebuild your software on
the Dawn nodes rather than try to run binaries previously compiled on CSD3.

Be aware that the software environment for Dawn is optimised for its hardware and
the binaries may fail to run on other CSD3 nodes, including cpu (*login-p*) and
icelake (*login-q*) login nodes. If you wish to recompile or test against this
new environment, we recommend requesting an interactive node with command:

```
sintr -t 01:00:00 -A YOURPROJECT-DAWN-GPU -p pvc9 -N 1 -c 24 --gres=gpu:1
```

The nodes are named according to the scheme *pvc-s-[1-256]*.

## Slurm partition[¶](#slurm-partition "Permalink to this headline")

The PVC (pvc-s) nodes are in a new **pvc9** Slurm partition.

Dawn Slurm projects follow the CSD3 naming convention for GPU projects and contain
units of GPU hours. Additionally Dawn project names follow the pattern NAME-DAWN-GPU.

> The pvc-s nodes have **96 cpus** (1 cpu = 1 core), and 1024 GiB of RAM. This
> means that Slurm will allocate **24 cpus per GPU**.

## Recommendations for running on Dawn[¶](#recommendations-for-running-on-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

The resource limits are currently set to a maximum of 64 GPUs per user with a
maximum wallclock time of 36 hours per job.

These limits should be regarded as provisional and may be revised.

### Default submission script for Dawn[¶](#default-submission-script-for-dawn "Permalink to this headline")

A template submission script will be provided soon.

To submit a job to the Dawn PVC partition, your batch script should look similar
to the following example:

```
#!/bin/bash -l
#SBATCH --job-name=my-batch-job
#SBATCH --account=<your Dawn SLURM project>
#SBATCH --partition=pvc9 # Dawn PVC partition
#SBATCH -n 4   # Number of tasks (usually number of MPI ranks)
#SBATCH -c 24  # Number of cores per task
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1 # Number of requested GPUs per node

module purge
module load rhel9/default-dawn
# Set up environment below for example by loading more modules

srun <your_application>
```

### Jobs requiring N GPUs where N < 4[¶](#jobs-requiring-n-gpus-where-n-4 "Permalink to this headline")

Although there are 4 GPUs in each node it is possible to request fewer than this, e.g. to request 3 GPUs use:

```
#SBATCH --nodes=1
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:3
#SBATCH -p pvc9
```

### Jobs requiring multiple nodes[¶](#jobs-requiring-multiple-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

Multi-node jobs need to request 4 GPUs per node, i.e.:

```
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:4
```

### Jobs requiring MPI[¶](#jobs-requiring-mpi "Permalink to this headline")

We currently recommend using the [Intel MPI Library](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/mpi-library.html) provided by the oneAPI toolkit:

```
module av intel-oneapi-mpi
```

To use GPU Aware MPI and allow passing device buffers to MPI calls, set the `I_MPI_OFFLOAD`
environment variable to `1` in your submission script:

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD=1
```

If you are sure that your code only involves buffers of the same type (e.g.
only GPU buffers or only host buffers) in a single
MPI operation you can further optimize MPI communication between GPUs by setting

```
export I_MPI_OFFLOAD_SYMMETRIC=1
```

This will disable handling of MPI communication between GPU buffers and host buffers.

### Multithreading jobs[¶](#multithreading-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

If your code uses multithreading (e.g. host-based OpenMP), you will need to
specify the number of threads per process in your Slurm batch script using the
cores-per-task parameter. For example, to run a hybrid MPI-OpenMP application
using 24 processes and 4 threads per task:

```
#SBATCH -n 24 # or --ntasks
#SBATCH -c 4 # or --cores-per-task
```

If you do *not* specify the cores-per-task parameter Slurm will pin the
threads to the same core, reducing performance.

### Recommended Compilers[¶](#recommended-compilers "Permalink to this headline")

We recommend using the [Intel oneAPI](https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/developer/tools/oneapi/overview.html) compilers

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the password sensibly, which in the age of automated cracking programs unfortunately means the following:

* Use at least 15 characters
* Use a mixture of upper and lower case letters, numbers and at least one non-alphanumeric character
* Do not use dictionary words, common proper nouns or simple rearrangements of these
* Do not use family names, user identifiers, car registrations, media references, …
* Do not re-use a password in use on another system (this is for damage limitation in case of a compromise somewhere).

Tip

If the UIS Password does not appear to work, please visit the [UIS Password Management page](https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/) and change your password. This will propagate your UIS Password to other systems such as BLUE AD which we use (it is permissible to change the password to itself provided the strength indicator shows GREEN).

Passwords should be treated like credit card numbers (and not left around, emailed or shared etc). The above rules are similar to those which apply to systems elsewhere.

## Filesystems[¶](#filesystems "Permalink to this headline")

Please see [here](/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html#summary-of-available-filesystems) for a summary of available filesystems and the rules governing them.

## Modules[¶](#modules "Permalink to this headline")

We use the modules environment extensively. A module can for instance be associated with a particular version of Intel compiler, or different MPI libraries etc. Loading a module establishes the environment required to find the related include and library files at compile-time and run-time.

By

# Getting Support on CSD3[¶](#getting-support-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The University of Cambridge provides support for users on CSD3 and can be contacted via email at support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This will open a ticket with the support team.

Tip

Each support request should be submitted as a *brand new* email to the address above. If you reopen a previously closed ticket, your request may be missed because it won’t reach the active service desk agent.

To help us help you, please provide as much information as possible in your ticket. At a minimum, please include:

* Your name
* Your account/PI
* your job submission script
* the working directories or paths you are using (e.g. `/rds/user/$USER/hpc-work/foo/bar/`), and
* as much detail as possible on what you are working on, what you have tried, and error messages you receive.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. We have compiled in this section some suggestions on how to safely work with these tools as well as how to engage the service desk.

Before acting on any AI-generated advice, please take a moment to review our guidance below on how to use these tools safely and responsibly.

Note

Much of the advice that follows is equally applicable to non-AI generated content found on the internet.

Please consult the following advice:

* **Understand the limitations:** AI chatbots do not have the same level of insight or familiarity with CSD3/Dawn as the human support team who maintain these systems.
* **Verify before you act:** Do not copy and run commands or instructions provided by AI models without review. Be especially cautious with:
  + Suggestions to use `sudo` commands or any other privilege escalation routes
  + Software installation steps that may be inappropriate or incompatible with our hardware/software stack(s)
  + Requests for login or sensitive credentials
* **Be security conscious:**
  + **Be aware of data retention:** Most AI assistants/websites retain or process data externally. Anything you share could be stored or used to improve the model; treat prompts as public information.
  + **Avoid sharing system-specific details:** Do not include configuration files, system paths, network layouts, or architecture diagrams that could expose internal infrastructure details.
  + **Do not upload logs or files directly:** These may contain sensitive metadata, user data, or internal identifiers that should remain confidential.

Tip

If you need to illustrate a problem, replace identifying information (e.g., usernames, project codes, or directory paths) with placeholders such as `<user>`, `<project>`, or `<path>`.

* **Protect your information:** Never include sensitive or identifying data in your prompts. This includes:
  + Account names
  + Passwords
  + SSH/API keys or other sensitive passcodes
  + IP addresses or hostnames
  + Any data protected under institutional, legal or intellectual property confidentiality policies

Attention

**If you think you have shared sensitive information:** make sure to reset the exposed credentials and let us know by contacting support@hpc.cam.ac.uk. In your e-mail, please provide a description of what happened, including as much relevant information as possible.

### What Can I Do?[¶](#what-can-i-do "Permalink to this headline")

The following items should help offer some clarity on how to engage with the CSD3/Dawn support team. Please consult and follow this advice.

1. **Communicate clearly with support:** when contacting the Service Desk, describe your original issue rather than the diagnosis or solution suggested by an AI assistant. Be sure to include the **full context** of the problem in your ticket.
2. **Be explicit:** use a descriptive subject line. In the body of your email, provide as much relevant information as possible and avoid pasting errors or log files without context. Users should include any error or log files, submission scripts, error messages, and any relevant commands used that would help the support team to retrace your steps and diagnose the fault.
3. **Cite AI use when relevant:** if AI suggestions informed your troubleshooting, mention this to Service Desk staff so they can identify and correct any misinformation. You won’t be penalised for telling us you’ve used AI to troubleshoot your problem.
4. **Prioritise official documentation:** treat AI advice as a potential aid, not an authority. The official CSD3/Dawn documentation and Service Desk are your primary sources of truth and troubleshooting for all technical issues.
5. **Contact us

Hello,

Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Module Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has requested the latest version of BLAST, noting that the current version (2.11) is incomplete. There is evidence that a newer version (2.9.0) exists but is not visible by default [3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the availability of the latest BLAST version.
2. If available, provide the user with the module load command for the latest version.
3. If not available, escalate to the appropriate team for installation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your request. We are aware that the current BLAST module (2.11) is incomplete. There is a newer version (2.9.0) that might address your needs. Please try the following command to load the newer version:

```
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr
```

If this version does not meet your requirements, please let us know, and we will escalate the request for further assistance.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the provided module path is correct and accessible to the user.
- If the module is not available, escalate the request to the appropriate team for installation.

# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4
2) rhel7/global                   7) vgl/2.5.1/64                  12) intel/libs/idb/2017.4         17) rhel7/default-peta4
3) spack/current                  8) singularity/current           13) intel/libs/tbb/2017.4
4) dot                            9) intel/compilers/2017.4        14) intel/libs/ipp/2017.4
5) java/jdk1.8.0_45              10) intel/mkl/2017.4              15) intel/libs/daal/2017.4
```

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4
2) rhel7/global                   7) vgl/2.5.1/64                  12) intel/libs/idb/2017.4         17) rhel7/default-peta4
3) spack/current                  8) singularity/current           13) intel/libs/tbb/2017.4
4) dot                            9) intel/compilers/2017.4        14) intel/libs/ipp/2017.4
5) java/jdk1.8.0_45              10) intel/mkl/2017.4              15) intel/libs/daal/2017.4
```

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

This command can be added in the .bashrc file for accessing the private modules

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue is related to activating a Conda environment within a Slurm batch job. The user's script correctly loads the Miniconda module and sources the .bashrc file, but the Conda environment activation does not persist within the job. This is a common issue with Conda environments in non-interactive shells [1][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Ensure the user's script includes the `conda init` command to initialize Conda in the non-interactive shell [3].
2. Verify that the script sources the .bashrc file after initializing Conda [3].
3. Confirm that the Conda environment is activated correctly within the script.

ACTION
Dear Stefanos,

Thank you for your query. The issue you're experiencing is common when activating Conda environments in non-interactive shells, such as Slurm batch jobs. To resolve this, please make the following adjustments to your Slurm script:

1. Ensure you include the `conda init` command to initialize Conda in the non-interactive shell.
2. Source the .bashrc file after initializing Conda.
3. Activate the Conda environment.

Here is an updated version of your script:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
module load miniconda

# Initialize Conda
conda init --all

# Source the .bashrc file
source ~/.bashrc

# Activate the environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

Please try running this updated script and let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the `conda init` command is included to properly initialize Conda in non-interactive shells [3].
- Always source the .bashrc file after initializing Conda to ensure the environment is correctly set up [3].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-16898::chunk::0000
[3] : HPCSSUP-52206::chunk::0001

# Using Python[¶](#using-python "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

You can also verify the current version of conda with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda --version
conda 4.7.12
```

If this is the first time using Anaconda Python then it is important to
run the first time `conda init` command to correctly prepare your shell
environemnt for using the full suite of `conda` commands. This only needs
to be done once.

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ which conda
/usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/conda
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda init
[... list of modifications not made ...]
modified      /home/user/.bashrc

==> For changes to take effect, close and re-open your current shell. <==
```

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate`

# Using Python[¶](#using-python "Permalink to this headline")

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

You can also verify the current version of conda with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda --version
conda 4.7.12
```

If this is the first time using Anaconda Python then it is important to
run the first time `conda init` command to correctly prepare your shell
environemnt for using the full suite of `conda` commands. This only needs
to be done once.

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ which conda
/usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/conda
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ conda init
[... list of modifications not made ...]
modified      /home/user/.bashrc

==> For changes to take effect, close and re-open your current shell. <==
```

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present

```

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`

Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

[TICKET_SUMMARY] Slow Conda environment setup and activation on CSD3

[INITIAL_DESCRIPTION]
Dear HPC Support Team,
I am a PhD student in the Engineering Department, division F, have been experiencing some significant delays with conda environment setup and activation on CSD3, and am attaching details below which I hope can be helpful in diagnosing the issue.
1. Environment build failures
I had to make three attempts to build a conda environment with PyTorch 2.8.0 and pyannote.audio 4.0.3. The first two jobs timed out despite using very little CPU or memory. The bottleneck appears to be network I/O when downloading packages. Here are the details:
Job ID
State
Wall-clock
CPU Used
Memory Used

TIMEOUT (killed during "Verifying transaction")
02:00:11
00:01:22 (0.28%)
357 MB (2.18%)
21596158
TIMEOUT (killed during "Installing pip dependencies")
04:00:08
00:02:00 (0.21%)
508 MB (3.10%)
21629759
COMPLETED
00:07:36
00:01:17 (3.38%)
278 MB (1.70%)
The third job completed quickly because the packages were already cached from the previous two attempts.
2. Conda activation and script runtime speed
Since last Thursday, activating conda environment has been notably slow. I had to remove the conda initialisation lines from my ~/.bashrc file in order to access the login node. I also tried requesting an interactive session and a CPU cluster node, but it could take 10–20 minutes to activate a conda environment. Things have been better today (around 3–5 minutes), but I thought it was still worth reporting in case the issue recurs.
Additionally, I have a testing script for the conda environment that finishes running in under 20 seconds (including initialisation time) on local machine, but takes 8–9 minutes to run on the login node or an interactive CPU session.
I am CC-ing colleagues in my team and @Jinghong Chen<[jc2124@cam.ac.uk](mailto:jc2124@cam.ac.uk)> and @Guangyu Yang<[gy266@cam.ac.uk](mailto:gy266@cam.ac.uk)>, whose team is experiencing similar conda-related slowness on the GPU cluster as well: a programme that is expected to finish in half an hour is taking over 6 hours to run.
I was wondering whether the slowness related to network I/O and conda is expected, and whether there is anything we are missing?
Many thanks and I look forward to hearing from you!
Best regards,
Shilin

# PyTorch[¶](#pytorch "Permalink to this headline")

The PyTorch [website](https://pytorch.org) describes the library as:

> Tensors and Dynamic neural networks in Python with strong GPU acceleration.

## Setup PyTorch on CSD3[¶](#setup-pytorch-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The python installation will depend on the target partition (CPU or GPU).
To run Pytorch on a CPU partition (cclake, icelake), please do the following:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl # recommended for all RHEL8 CPU partitions (cclake, icelake, sapphire)
module load python/3.11.9/gcc/nptrdpll

# create and activate a virtual environment:

python -m venv ./pytorch-cclake-env
source ./pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env (instructions from Intel: https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/installation?platform=cpu&version=v2.7.0%2Bcpu&os=linux%2Fwsl2&package=pip)
python -m pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
python -m pip install intel-extension-for-pytorch
python -m pip install oneccl_bind_pt --extra-index-url https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/release-whl/stable/cpu/us/
```

To run Pytorch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m venv ./pytorch-ampere-env
source ./pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
