As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cga442%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C983f06fd84ac47a4a95108db9b9356d4%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638274829346491872%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=0%2FpMVyrjANtUCAgy6nDFjhYZ4m74kmynLUrCgbuJhdE%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cga442%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C983f06fd84ac47a4a95108db9b9356d4%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638274829346491872%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=0%2FpMVyrjANtUCAgy6nDFjhYZ4m74kmynLUrCgbuJhdE%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the password sensibly, which in the age of automated cracking programs unfortunately means the following:

* Use at least 15 characters
* Use a mixture of upper and lower case letters, numbers and at least one non-alphanumeric character
* Do not use dictionary words, common proper nouns or simple rearrangements of these
* Do not use family names, user identifiers, car registrations, media references, …
* Do not re-use a password in use on another system (this is for damage limitation in case of a compromise somewhere).

Tip

If the UIS Password does not appear to work, please visit the [UIS Password Management page](https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/) and change your password. This will propagate your UIS Password to other systems such as BLUE AD which we use (it is permissible to change the password to itself provided the strength indicator shows GREEN).

Passwords should be treated like credit card numbers (and not left around, emailed or shared etc). The above rules are similar to those which apply to systems elsewhere.

## Filesystems[¶](#filesystems "Permalink to this headline")

Please see [here](/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html#summary-of-available-filesystems) for a summary of available filesystems and the rules governing them.

## Modules[¶](#modules "Permalink to this headline")

We use the modules environment extensively. A module can for instance be associated with a particular version of Intel compiler, or different MPI libraries etc. Loading a module establishes the environment required to find the related include and library files at compile-time and run-time.

user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing an issue with RStudio where they are logged out during an interactive session and cannot log back in using their HPC credentials. This issue has been reported previously and may require technical intervention [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the user's account and session details.
2. Verify if the user is following the correct procedure to start and reconnect to an RStudio session [2].
3. Check if there are any ongoing issues with the RStudio server that might be causing the problem [1][3].
4. If the issue persists, escalate to the technical team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact URL and error message you are seeing when trying to log back in?
- Have you tried logging in using different browsers or clearing your browser cache?
- Are there any specific steps you are following to start and reconnect to the RStudio session?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for reaching out. We understand that you are experiencing issues with logging back into your RStudio session after being logged out. This issue has been reported previously, and we are working to resolve it.

Please follow these steps to ensure you are correctly starting and reconnecting to your RStudio session:
1. Start a new RStudio session from the `Interactive Apps` dropdown in the HPC portal [2].
2. Fill in the job details and press the `Launch` button.
3. Once the job is running, press the `Connect to RStudio Server` button.

If you continue to experience issues, please provide the exact URL and error message you are seeing when trying to log back in. Additionally, try logging in using different browsers or clearing your browser cache.

We will escalate this issue to our technical team if necessary.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's credentials and session details are handled securely.
- Follow the documented procedures for starting and reconnecting to RStudio sessions [2].

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-54003::chunk::0000
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html::chunk::0003
[3] : HPCSSUP-74454::chunk::0000

8. Start a new logbook by clicking on the button “New” on the right-hand side:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-envs.jpg)

Your virtual environment should be listed under the selected `Python3` as shown in the above illustration. Selecting the virtual environment as kernel will give you access to the python packages you have installed in the virtual environment.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-with-virtual-env.png)

#### Delisting virtual environments[¶](#delisting-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To remove unwanted kernels from the Jupyter notebook:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. At the prompt:

   ```
   module load jupyterlab/3
   jupyter kernelspec list
   ```
3. The last command should give you the same list of kernels as the jupyter notebook app. Select the kernel to be deleted, then type:

   ```
   jupyter kernelspec uninstall <myenv>
   ```

### RStudio server: Csd3[¶](#rstudio-server-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is an open-source, integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It includes a console, syntax-highlighting editor that supports direct code execution, and tools for plotting, history, debugging, and workspace management.

#### Starting RStudio on CSD3[¶](#starting-rstudio-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

From the `Interactive Apps` dropdown, select `RStudio server: Csd3`. This should open a job form as shown in the following image:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-start.png)

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-newproject.png)

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-session.png)

#### Further Reading:[¶](#further-reading "Permalink to this headline")

## Reference[¶](#reference "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web is based on [Open OnDemand](https://openondemand.org/)

This documentation is based on Ohio Supercomputer Center user documentation for their Open OnDemand deployment (OSC OnDemand).

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

to `~/.R/Makevars`.

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-session.png)

#### Further Reading:[¶](#further-reading "Permalink to this headline")

## Reference[¶](#reference "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web is based on [Open OnDemand](https://openondemand.org/)

This documentation is based on Ohio Supercomputer Center user documentation for their Open OnDemand deployment (OSC OnDemand).

More detail can be found at:

### App Developers[¶](#app-developers "Permalink to this headline")

If you’d like to develop your own app through OnDemand, see the link below for detailed instructions.

# R and Rscript[¶](#r-and-rscript "Permalink to this headline")

Various versions of the R environment are available on CSD3. The latest
versions are `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/7dubq4gn` (GTK library support), `r/4.1.3/gcc/intel-oneapi-mkl/wwmppxe3` (vanilla) on the `icelake` partition and `r/4.4.0/gcc/3z5n2tph` on the `cclake` partition.

R can be run interactively by simply calling `R`, which will give a standard
R shell. This can be used for testing or setting up larger runs. However for
use inside the job scheduler it will generally be more effective to record the
R script in a file `analysis.R` and call it from inside a slurm batch script
`Rscript analysis.R`. For more examples of how to use slurm and write an
appropriate batch script please see [Running](../user-guide/quickstart.html#running).

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

to `~/.R/Makevars`.

Dear support team
I am having trouble accessing the RFS service for my lab group (TP group, Condensed Matter Physics, Maxwell Centre). It has already been mapped, and it was working fine, but today it asked me for the password of my username (of the computer I was using). As you know, those are generated tokens. So, I generated a new one for the computer I am using, but it does not accept it. How can we fix that? It is urgent that I have access to my data.
Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics
University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RFS Access
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues accessing the RFS service despite having a valid token. This issue has been reported before and may require further investigation [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's token and access permissions to the RFS service.
2. Check the network connection and ensure the ethernet port is functioning correctly [1].
3. If the issue persists, escalate to the appropriate technical team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried accessing the RFS service from a different computer or network?
- Can you provide the exact error message you are receiving?
- Have you recently changed your password or token?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patricia,

Thank you for reaching out. We understand that you are having trouble accessing the RFS service for your lab group. We will need to verify your token and access permissions to resolve this issue.

Please try the following steps:
1. Ensure that your token is valid and has not expired.
2. Check your network connection and make sure the ethernet port is functioning correctly.
3. If the issue persists, please provide the exact error message you are receiving.

We will escalate this issue to the appropriate technical team if necessary. Please let us know if you have any further questions or concerns.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's token and access permissions are verified before proceeding with further troubleshooting steps [1].
- If the issue cannot be resolved through basic troubleshooting, escalate to the appropriate technical team for further investigation.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-38159::chunk::0000

My name is Victor, and I’m currently a summer student in PDN’s Library lab (anatomy building, CRSid vgp23). I’ve been using the eplab RFS (directory rfs-ekp25-eplab) for the past few weeks without issue, however yesterday I suddenly stopped being able to access any of it (and currently have no internet access via the same ethernet port I’ve been using until now, port number 105). I’ve tried to forget the network drive connection and re-connect the way I first did, but I keep getting an incorrect user name or password error when trying to sign in with any of my UIS tokens (despite them working fine to sign into eduroam). Please let me know if you need any additional information, I would be happy to supply it. Thank you in advance! 
  
Kind regards, 
  
Victor

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the password sensibly, which in the age of automated cracking programs unfortunately means the following:

* Use at least 15 characters
* Use a mixture of upper and lower case letters, numbers and at least one non-alphanumeric character
* Do not use dictionary words, common proper nouns or simple rearrangements of these
* Do not use family names, user identifiers, car registrations, media references, …
* Do not re-use a password in use on another system (this is for damage limitation in case of a compromise somewhere).

Tip

If the UIS Password does not appear to work, please visit the [UIS Password Management page](https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/) and change your password. This will propagate your UIS Password to other systems such as BLUE AD which we use (it is permissible to change the password to itself provided the strength indicator shows GREEN).

Passwords should be treated like credit card numbers (and not left around, emailed or shared etc). The above rules are similar to those which apply to systems elsewhere.

## Filesystems[¶](#filesystems "Permalink to this headline")

Please see [here](/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html#summary-of-available-filesystems) for a summary of available filesystems and the rules governing them.

## Modules[¶](#modules "Permalink to this headline")

We use the modules environment extensively. A module can for instance be associated with a particular version of Intel compiler, or different MPI libraries etc. Loading a module establishes the environment required to find the related include and library files at compile-time and run-time.

By

# User Dawn Access[¶](#user-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## Users Accessing Portal[¶](#users-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (Identity of Last Resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-user.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

### **University Login (MyAccessID)**

Step 1: Click the “MyAccessID” button to authenticate using your university credentials.

[![Access ID Login Option](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)](../_images/raven_and_access_id.png)

If this is your first time accessing MyAccessID, please ensure that you provide the necessary details and take a moment to review their policy. If you have further issues with using MyAccessID and you have a university email address, please contact your local IT department to ensure you are able to use MyAccessID.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/access_id_registration.png)](../_images/access_id_registration.png)

Step 2: Start typing in the name of your university/institution in the “login with” box. You will hopefully see your university appear in the list of options.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/access_id_empty_uni.png)](../_images/access_id_empty_uni.png)

Step 3: Click on your university - this will take you to your normal university login page. Log in as you would normally. You will then be redirected back to the Cambridge access portal once you have logged in using your university credentials.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/access_id_cam_uni.png)](../_images/access_id_cam_uni.png)

Step 4: After entering your university credentials, you will be prompted to share your MyAccessID account information with our service. Please read through the Data Privacy information and give your consent for access by clicking “Accept” to proceed.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/cam_login_page.png)](../_images/cam_login_page.png)
[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/cam_login_request_access.png)](../_images/cam_login_request_access.png)

Step 5: To secure your account, you must set up Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Scan the QR code using your authentication app, configure it on your phone, and enter the one-time code to complete the setup.

[![Access ID User Registration](../_images/cam_login_mfa.png)](../_images/cam_login_mfa.png)

You will now have a code in your authenticator app labelled “Waldur” (Please do not delete this code as you will need to use it each time you access the Dawn access portal. For more information on MFA please see our user documentation page:https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html)

You will now have access to the portal. Proceed to the next section [Accepting the Invitation](https://.docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-user.html#accepting-the-invitation).

### Identity of Last Resort - Accessing without MyAccessID[¶](#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid "Permalink to this headline")

If you are experiencing difficulties using MyAccessID because you cannot find your organisation within the system, it is crucial to take the necessary steps to address this issue. In such a case, you will need to request a local account from our service desk.

At the bottom of the page, you will find a support option. Please click on it and copy the email address: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk .

[![Keycloak_signin_support](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)

When composing your email, make sure to include the title “Identity of last resort on Cambridge Access Portal” in the subject line. This will help the support team to identify your request promptly.

Please provide the name of your organisation along with the email address that will be used to access the Portal.

[![Support_email_draft](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)

The service desk team will process your ticket and create your local account in our Keycloak system. You will receive an email

user_input:
To Whom It May Concern,
For context, I have been using the Remote Desktop interactive sessions via the web interface for about a year.  As of this morning, requesting a desktop no longer provides the “Launch Remote Desktop” button.  Please see the attached screenshot. I expected to see the “Launch Remote Desktop” button as shown in the second screenshot. I would appreciate any insights or support. Thank you.
-Pete
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support

Once the desktop is ready to be used, your session manager will show a job which looks like the image below:

The remaining options shown under `Interactive Apps` will use the same functionality to launch specific applications, rather than full desktops.

### MATLAB[¶](#matlab "Permalink to this headline")

MATLAB allows matrix manipulations, plotting of functions and data, implementation of algorithms, creation of user interfaces, and interfacing with programs written in other languages, including C, C++, C#, Java, Fortran and Python.

#### Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#accessing-through-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` drop-down in the Login-Web web portal as shown in the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-menu.png)

After selecting MATLAB, you will arrive at this page:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-select.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your MATLAB session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. If you’d like to receive an email notifying you of when this has happened, please select `I would like to receive an email when the session starts`. After clicking `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-launch.png)

To access the interface you can choose from `Launch MATLAB` to reach the application as shown below and use it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share the application interface with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

#### Using MATLAB[¶](#using-matlab "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have entered your session, you should see a new tab. Allow MATLAB a moment to start up, then your screen should eventually look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab.png)

This is a typical MATLAB interface that should be familiar:

* Current Folder — Access your files.
* Command Window — Enter commands at the command line, indicated by the prompt (>>).
* Workspace — Explore data that you create or import from files.

The desktop also includes these applications:
:   * Web Browser
    * Terminal Emulator
    * File Manager

**Further Reading**: [MathWorks support page](https://www.mathworks.com/support/learn-with-matlab-tutorials.html)

### Jupyter Notebook[¶](#jupyter-notebook "Permalink to this headline")

The Jupyter Notebook is an open-source web application that allows you to create and share documents that contain live code, equations, visualisations and narrative text. Uses include: data cleaning and transformation, numerical simulation, statistical modeling, data visualisation, machine learning, and much more.

#### Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#jupyter-access-loginweb "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` dropdown in the Login-Web web portal. After selecting Jupyter, you will arrive at this page:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-menu.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your Jupyter session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. After you click `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-launch.png)

You can choose `Connect to Jupyter` to start your session and create a `New` Python 3 notebook.

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter-new.png)

This is a new notebook with a familiar interface:

![Jupyter Notebook](../_images/ondemand-apps-jupyter.png)

#### Adding virtual environments[¶](#adding-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

To use your own virtual environments in the Jupyter Notebook app, you need to add them externally. Use the following steps to do so:

1. Use the `Remote Desktop` application to log to your CSD3 account, then start a `Terminal`.
2. If needed, create a new virtual environment using the commands described in the section [Setup Jupyter on CSD3](../software-packages/jupyter.html#setup-jupyter-on-csd3)
3. **Activate the virtual environment** (otherwise, your installed packages won’t be registered)
4. Deploy the python module **ipykernel** with the command line:

   ```
   pip install ipykernel
   ```
5. Add your virtual environment to the Jupyter Notebook as new kernel, with the command line:

   ```
   ipython kernel install --user --name=<virtual environment name>
   ```
6. Logout.
7. Start or refresh your jupyter notebook session, as described in the previous section [Accessing through

[cid:8dcc24fc-0af4-4c8e-8b34-11cb77cfcc35]
[cid:f91e188a-0541-4cc6-a42a-1e059feb1a00]

and select `Edit` from the main menu. This will open up a text editor in a new tab.

## Job Management[¶](#job-management "Permalink to this headline")

This function allows you to create, edit, submit, and monitor jobs. To access the job management tools, use the `Jobs` menu.

![Jobs menu open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-menu.png)

Login-Web provides two related job management tools: one allows you to create and submit jobs via your web browser, and the other allows you to monitor queued and running jobs.

### Job Composer[¶](#job-composer "Permalink to this headline")

Selecting `Job Composer` in the `Jobs` menu will open an application which allows you to inspect the results of jobs submitted, and to create new jobs and submit them to the cluster via this tool.

![Jobs menu open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-composer.png)

#### New Job[¶](#new-job "Permalink to this headline")

Use the following steps in order to create a job:

1. Create a new job by using an existing job template by clicking `+New Job` and then `From Template` (or alternatively `From Default Template`). Select the job template to copy, then click `Create New Job`. The template directory will be copied and a new job row will be added to the top of the jobs table on the page titled `Jobs`:

> ![Create new Job](../_images/ondemand-jobs-create.png)

2. Select the job, then modify and/or add any files to this job by clicking `Edit Files`:

> ![Edit files](../_images/ondemand-jobs-edit-files.png)

3. Select the job and click `Job Options` to change the cluster, job name, or job script, if necessary:

> ![Options](../_images/ondemand-jobs-options.png)

4. At this point, you can submit the job using the green `Submit` button from the `Jobs` page; or return to this job later to modify it further. You can also monitor the job status here. Currently you must reload the page to see job status changes.

### Job Management[¶](#id1 "Permalink to this headline")

On the main screen for `Jobs`, click on a job to examine it.

#### Submit Job[¶](#submit-job "Permalink to this headline")

If the job has not been submitted, the `Green Submit` button will be active, and will submit the selected job to the queue.

#### Stop Job[¶](#stop-job "Permalink to this headline")

The `Stop` button will allow you to kill a running job.

#### Delete Job[¶](#delete-job "Permalink to this headline")

The `Delete` button will allow you to delete a job.

### Active Jobs[¶](#active-jobs "Permalink to this headline")

The `Active Jobs` application will show all of your jobs currently in the queue (running or queued), regardless of how the jobs were submitted. You can click the button at the top to switch from:

> * All jobs submitted from your CSD3 user account
> * All jobs submitted by all CSD3 users

You can also use the `Filter` tool to search jobs based on system, status (running, queued, or held), CSD3 user account, etc.

![Jobs menu open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-listing.png)

### Job Inspection[¶](#job-inspection "Permalink to this headline")

Clicking the arrow to the left of each job will show more information about the job.

![Jobs inspection open](../_images/ondemand-jobs-inspection.png)

## Shell Access[¶](#shell-access "Permalink to this headline")

Login-Web provides the ability to SSH to a CSD3 login node from inside your web browser. To obtain a terminal, select the `Clusters` menu and choose the desired flavour of login node.

![Shell menu open](../_images/ondemand-shell-menu.png)

Note

Please note that this action will open a “pop-up” window. If your browser is configured to block pop-ups, you will not see the terminal.

## Interactive Apps[¶](#interactive-apps "Permalink to this headline")

![Apps menu open](../_images/ondemand-apps-menu.png)

This function launches a browser-based remote Linux desktop on which to run GUI applications, without the hassle or performance problems associated with X11 forwarding or custom VNC clients. To use this feature, please select an item from under Desktops, GUIs or Servers from the `Interactive Apps` menu.

### Remote Desktop[¶](#remote-desktop "Permalink to this headline")

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-select.png)

This option will launch a full remote desktop in a browser tab. Select `Remote Desktop` and you will arrive on the page shown below. Click `Launch`. You may need to wait for a few minutes for the application to launch.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-wait.png)

To access the desktop you can choose from `Launch Remote Desktop` to reach your desktop as shown below and interact with it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share your desktop with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-starting.png)
![Remote

Hello,
I hope you're well.
I'm a current PhD student and was just made a Data user on the Research Data Store for the Genetics_HPC partition by a collaborator. Upon opening the authentication link, I completely mistakenly clicked "Do not agree", rather than "Agree". The latter was certainly my intention, and now cannot access the drive.
Would there be any way to reverse this and reauthenticate?
Thank you,
Maria
Maria Jelen<[https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/](https://www.mrc-cbu.cam.ac.uk/people/maria.jelen/)> (she/her)
Second-Year PhD Student at 4D Lab<[https://www.astlelab.com/](https://www.astlelab.com/)>
Medical Research Council Cognition and Brain Sciences Unit
mbj26@<[maria.jelen@](mailto:maria.jelen@)>cam.ac.uk

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Access / Invitation Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user mistakenly declined the terms and conditions for accessing the Research Data Store, which is preventing them from accessing the drive. This issue has been previously documented and can be resolved by re-sending the invitation link [1].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Re-send the invitation link to the user to accept the terms and conditions.
2. If the issue persists, escalate to the Storage Services Team for further assistance [1].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Maria,

Thank you for reaching out. We can resolve this by re-sending the invitation link to accept the terms and conditions. Please check your email for the new link and follow the instructions to gain access to the Research Data Store.

If you encounter any further issues, please let us know.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user is re-sent the correct invitation link to avoid further access issues [1].
- Escalate to the Storage Services Team if the problem cannot be resolved through re-sending the link [1].

# User Dawn Access[¶](#user-dawn-access "Permalink to this headline")

## Users Accessing Portal[¶](#users-accessing-portal "Permalink to this headline")

**Invitation E-mail:**

You will receive an email from airr-admin@hpc.cam.ac.uk. This email will contain a link to access the portal; please click on that link to proceed.

[![RAC Invitation Email](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)](../_images/waldur_invitation_email.png)

You have two choices for your identity provider:

* University Login (MyAccessID)
* Other Login (Identity of Last Resort)

If you encounter any issues, please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk for assistance.

Make sure to select the option that corresponds to the email address where you received your invitation.

If your invitation was sent to your university account, choose the “Sign in with Keycloak” option. This will allow you to log in using your University credentials.
Otherwise, please proceed to the [Identity of Last Resort](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/waldur-user.html#identity-of-last-resort-accessing-without-myaccessid) section.

Dear HPC cam support,
I am a new user that has been added to the project and i already set up my
MFA auth with MyAccessID and can access the dashboard of the project.
I have linked my ssh public key through the dashboard, although the mail
that i received says to send the public also to this email address. Thus, i
attach it just in case is needed.
ssh-ed25519
AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIACYZwByA+c+iUfw7IQIy0u9my6yGu/FdXd1hnahrjYz
matteo.baldan.7@studenti.unipd.it
I got the message to "Select “Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop” and launch a
job on the pvc9 partition, requesting at least one GPU on one node, using
the above project.", but it seems that I am not able to find such

reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: DAWN Onboarding / SSH Access / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has been added to a DAWN project but is unsure how to access the service and what credentials to use. The correct onboarding path is to confirm whether the submitted public key has been linked to the DAWN account, complete the separate SSH MFA setup, then connect using the corresponding private key and the DAWN login host.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the public key submitted via the dashboard/email has been linked to the DAWN account.
2) Direct the user to the SSH MFA guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk
3) Provide the DAWN login command:
   `ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
4) Clarify that the SSH client must point to the private key paired with the public key they supplied.
5) Share the DAWN/PVC documentation:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/pvc.html

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Matteo,

If your public key has already been linked to the DAWN account, the next step is to complete the separate SSH MFA setup and then connect using:
`ssh dn-bald1@login-dawn.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

If your SSH client asks for authentication, make sure it is using the private key that matches the public key you sent us.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: User Onboarding / Access Issues
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has successfully set up MFA and linked their SSH public key but is having trouble finding the "Interactive Apps/Remote Desktop" option and is unsure about their SSH credentials.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm that the user's SSH public key has been added to the system.
2. Provide instructions for setting up an additional TOTP for SSH connections.
3. Direct the user to the documentation for launching interactive apps via the web interface.

ACTION
Hello Matteo,

Thank you for your message. Your SSH public key has been received and will be added to the system. To create an additional TOTP that will work with SSH connections, please run the following command:

```
ssh dn-bela1@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

You will be presented with a QR code to show your authenticator application. Note that the SSH TOTP is distinct from any others you may have installed on your authenticator: do not delete your existing TOTP. For further guidance, please follow the documentation here: [MFA Guidance](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) [1][2].

Regarding the interactive desktop, please follow the instructions on this page: [Interactive Apps](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html#interactive-apps) [1].

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user's SSH public key is correctly added to the system.
- Provide clear instructions for setting up MFA and accessing interactive apps.
- Do not share passwords or sensitive information via email.

REFERENCE KEY
[1] : HPCSSUP-101079::chunk::0001
[2] : https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/quickstart.html::chunk::0000

# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Note that the security of both users’ data and the service itself depends strongly on choosing the password sensibly, which in the age of automated cracking programs unfortunately means the following:

* Use at least 15 characters
* Use a mixture of upper and lower case letters, numbers and at least one non-alphanumeric character
* Do not use dictionary words, common proper nouns or simple rearrangements of these
* Do not use family names, user identifiers, car registrations, media references, …
* Do not re-use a password in use on another system (this is for damage limitation in case of a compromise somewhere).

Tip

If the UIS Password does not appear to work, please visit the [UIS Password Management page](https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/) and change your password. This will propagate your UIS Password to other systems such as BLUE AD which we use (it is permissible to change the password to itself provided the strength indicator shows GREEN).

Passwords should be treated like credit card numbers (and not left around, emailed or shared etc). The above rules are similar to those which apply to systems elsewhere.

## Filesystems[¶](#filesystems "Permalink to this headline")

Please see [here](/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html#summary-of-available-filesystems) for a summary of available filesystems and the rules governing them.

## Modules[¶](#modules "Permalink to this headline")

We use the modules environment extensively. A module can for instance be associated with a particular version of Intel compiler, or different MPI libraries etc. Loading a module establishes the environment required to find the related include and library files at compile-time and run-time.

By
